title: Can Machines Think?
caption: Alan Turing, photographed in 1951 — a year after he reframed "can machines think?" as the imitation game.

overview.text: Alan Turing sidestepped the unanswerable question "can machines think?" and replaced it with a game. If a human interrogator, exchanging typed messages, cannot reliably tell a machine from a person, then — Turing argued — we may as well say the machine thinks. The paper also anticipated and rebutted nine objections to the idea of machine intelligence.

deep.text: Turing's "imitation game" is a behavioural test: it defines intelligence operationally, by indistinguishable performance, rather than by internal mechanism. This functionalist move still frames debates today — from chatbots to large language models — about whether fluent output is evidence of genuine understanding.

challenge.prompt: What does the "imitation game" actually test?
options: Whether a machine can feel emotion
Whether a machine's responses are indistinguishable from a human's
Whether a machine can beat a human at chess
Whether a machine can solve any mathematical problem

feedback.correct: Right — Turing reframed "can machines think?" as a question about indistinguishable behaviour.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite — the test is about whether a machine's replies can be told apart from a person's.
